title: Buffer Zones

content.question: Where are you running too tight to absorb a normal shock?
sop: Identify where demand or load varies the most, cash flow, staff hours, calendar, inbox
Calculate your current capacity and average demand for that area (see calculation, page 5)
Add a 10-20% buffer on top of average demand, cash reserve, bench staff, open calendar slots
Set a trigger for when the buffer gets used (a bad month, a no-show, an emergency)
Review quarterly whether the buffer was too small, too big, or just right
content.explainer: A system sized exactly to average demand looks efficient right up until demand isn't average, which is most of the time, since averages hide the spikes. The buffer isn't wasted capacity; it's the difference between a bad week being an inconvenience and a bad week being a collapse.
questions: Where are you running too tight to absorb a normal shock?
What's the last time a normal, predictable disruption (a sick day, a late client, a slow month) knocked your whole system over?
If demand spiked 20% next week, which part of your business or life has zero room to absorb it?
content.equation_example: Capacity: 100 units/hours available. Average Demand: 80 units/hours typically used. Buffer = 100 minus 80 = 20 units. Higher buffer means more room to absorb an unexpected spike without breaking; a buffer near zero or negative means any normal fluctuation becomes a crisis.
content.closing_statement: Room to absorb shocks beats running at full capacity.

caption:
Principle Nº 209: Buffer Zones

Plan slack to handle uncertainty. A system sized exactly to average demand looks efficient right up until demand isn't average, which is most of the time, since averages hide the spikes.

Ask yourself: Where are you running too tight to absorb a normal shock?
